coredata.dc:title: Protecting black phosphorus with selectively adsorbed graphene quantum dot layers
coredata.dc:creator: Laxmi, Vijay
Dong, Weilong
Wang, Haoqiang
Qi, Dianyu
Hao, Qiaoyan
Ouyang, Zhengbiao
Ahmad, Waqas
Shah, Muhammad Najeeb Ullah
Yuan, Qunhui
Zhang, Wenjing
coredata.dc:description: Black phosphorus (BP) is an exciting material that possesses excellent electronic properties, such as high mobility, and thickness-dependent, easily tunable band gap, making it a promising candidate for applications in optoelectronics, energy storage and nano-photonics. However, the instability of mechanically exfoliated BP thin films under oxygen and humidity conditions is still a bottleneck in its practical applications. Herein, we choose a facile way to effectively protect BP by selectively adsorbing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on its surface as a protective layer. This strategy can prevent the degradation of the thin BP layer in the presence of oxygen and humidity. We systematically investigated the impact of GQDs absorption on BP surface via atomic force microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and electrical measurements. Our results show that GQDs could effectively protects the BP surface from exposure to atmospheric oxidants, resulting in significantly improved stability along with enhanced mobility and on/off ratio of corresponding field-effect transistors (FETs). Importantly, the electrical properties of optimized FETs could be retained for over 24 h even under heating at 100 ℃ in ambient conditions. This work offers a facile approach to efficiently prevent BP and other air-sensitive materials from environmental oxidants for their practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-dimensional
Black phosphorus
Graphene quantum dots
Stability
Field effect transistors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work at Shenzhen University ( SZU ) was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51472164 and 61805159 ), the 1000 Talents Program for Young Scientists of China, Shenzhen Peacock Plan (Grant No. KQTD2016053112042971 ), the Educational Commission of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2015KGJHZ006 and 2016KCXTD006 ), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2016B050501005 ), Natural Science Foundation of SZU (Grant No. 000050 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustrations of sample preparation: (I) peeled metal patterns on PDMS and BP nanosheets on silicon dioxide; (II) alignment; (III) BP with metal electrodes on it, and (IV) BP samples with selectively adsorbed GQDs on its surface. AFM images of BP nanosheets (b) without and (c) with GQDs; the slight difference in the thickness indicates that only a single layer of GQDs is adsorbed on BP surface. The inset in (c) is the TEM image of GQDs.

(a) Optical image of BP flake transferred to a TEM grid. (b) TEM image of multi-layered region of BP flake (~13–21 layers). (c) & (d) TEM images of the GQDs covered region of the BP flake at different resolution.

AFM images depicting degradation of BP surface with (a–d) and without (e-f) GQDs. BP surface after dropping GQDs (a) original, (b) after 3 days and (c, d) after 7 days. The red box zone in (d) indicates that GQDs can be influenced by AFM tip scanning. Bare BP surface (e) original, (f) after 3 days, (g) after 7 days (indicating the appearance of several oxide protrusions) and (h) after 13 days (revealing that the BP nanosheet almost vanished). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Raman measurements of BP samples. (a) Raman spectra of bare BP and BP covered with GQDs. Stored for 35 days (with GQDs) and 14 days (bare BP) separately. (b) The evolution of A2g peak of bare BP and BP with GQDs covered respect to the stored time. (c) Raman mapping of A2g peak of BP (465.15 cm−1) covered with GQDs stored for 35 days, the inset shows a corresponding AFM image. (d) Raman mapping of A2g peak of bare BP (465.15 cm−1) stored for 14 days, the inset presents a corresponding AFM image.

Electrical characterization of BP-based FETs: (a) AFM image of the GQDs protected BP device, the inset shows an optical image of the device. The area in red box is used for AFM characterization. (b) Isd–Vsd plots (Vsd from −1 to 1 V) of the device with increasing Vg from −80 to 50 V. (c) Transfer characteristics evolution (at Vsd = 1 V) of the BP device before (black curve) and after (other curves) depositing GQDs in linear scale for different storage time. The red curve is obtained directly after dropping GQDs on BP surface. (d) Transfer characteristics evolution (at Vsd = 1 V) of the unprotected BP device in linear scale for different storage time. (e) Extracted hole mobilities of the fabricated devices as a function of exposure time. (f) Estimated on/off ratio of the fabricated FETs versus exposure time. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Measurements of BP device under heating conditions: (a) AFM image of GQDs covered BP based FET device at room temperature. The AFM image of the device after heating at (b) 50 ℃ for 30 mins (the thickness of the device remained unchanged) (c) 100 ℃ for 30 mins (parts of GQDs moved away upon heating) and (d) 150 ℃ for 30 mins (only one GQDs layer was left on BP surface). (e) The corresponding transfer curves of the devices for each heating condition. (f) Transfer curves of long-term heated BP devices with (solid line) and without (dash line) GQDs.

head.ce:title: Protecting black phosphorus with selectively adsorbed graphene quantum dot layers

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Vijay Laxmi is the first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Black phosphorus (BP) is an exciting material that possesses excellent electronic properties, such as high mobility, and thickness-dependent, easily tunable band gap, making it a promising candidate for applications in optoelectronics, energy storage and nano-photonics. However, the instability of mechanically exfoliated BP thin films under oxygen and humidity conditions is still a bottleneck in its practical applications. Herein, we choose a facile way to effectively protect BP by selectively adsorbing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on its surface as a protective layer. This strategy can prevent the degradation of the thin BP layer in the presence of oxygen and humidity. We systematically investigated the impact of GQDs absorption on BP surface via atomic force microscopy, Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and electrical measurements. Our results show that GQDs could effectively protects the BP surface from exposure to atmospheric oxidants, resulting in significantly improved stability along with enhanced mobility and on/off ratio of corresponding field-effect transistors (FETs). Importantly, the electrical properties of optimized FETs could be retained for over 24 h even under heating at 100 ℃ in ambient conditions. This work offers a facile approach to efficiently prevent BP and other air-sensitive materials from environmental oxidants for their practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Black phosphorus (BP) has emerged as an exciting two-dimensional (2D) material that holds attractive optoelectronic properties. These puckered materials have distinctive crystalline symmetries and show various amazing properties, such as high carrier mobility, strong infrared responsivity, in-plane anisotropy and spontaneous electric polarization [1]. The bandgap of BP lies in the range of 0.3 to 2 eV from monolayer to bulk [2,3], thereby offering a broad spectral range which makes it a promising candidate for nanophotonic and nanoelectronics applications. BP has enormous diversity in applications in many fields, including storage system, energy generation as well as bio/chemical sensing [4–11]. Moreover, it is an auspicious material that is capable of overtaking graphene in digital applications [12,13]. The high p-type mobility and on/off current ratio mark it a more favourable candidate for p-channel field effect transistors (FETs), and high performance devices have been demonstrated by integrating BP with n-type MoS2 [14–19]. However, the exploration of BP for certain applications is largely restricted due to its instability in air. The chemical degradation in ambient conditions remains a major obstacle to its perspective applications.

Many efforts have been dedicated on understanding the degradation of BP flakes [20–29], and it is established that its degradation is caused by oxygen and water in the air as well as the photons which can accelerate the reaction [30]. However, water molecules only doped BP in absence of oxygen molecules and will catalyze oxidation of few-layer BP in a dark environment in the presence of oxygen. Meanwhile, the degradation of BP flakes is observed to occur particularly from P atoms locating at BP edges in oxygenated water, which demonstrates the importance of the role of oxygen during the oxidation [30–35]. Numerous strategies have been introduced to stabilize BP in ambient conditions. For instance, coatings (such as AlOx, BN layer, C8-BTBT, PDI) and plasma etching etc [36–41]. were performed onto the surface of BP, which prevent the contact between BP and humid air, leading to remarkably improved stability of BP. Meanwhile, organic molecules (e.g. 4-MBD and TiL4) and metal cations were also employed to react with the lone pair electrons of BP to form a covalent bond, which can reduce the surface potential of BP to improve its stability [28,39–41]. However, these strategies have several issues, such as lack of long-term protection, limiting the large scale utilization, require expensive equipment with complex operating process, or high doping of BP that could alter its original ambipolar transport characteristics. In this scenario, a facile and cost-effective method with long-term protection, compatible for large scale usage, and preserving the intrinsic properties of BP is imperative.

To develop a straightforward approach for long-term protection of BP, herein, we choose graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form a protective layer, which can be selectively adsorbed on the top of BP surface. By protecting BP with GQDs, we demonstrate highly stable BP-based FETs, which exhibit stability for more than one month. GQDs were physically absorbed as a passivation layer to protect the BP surface, without scarifying its ambipolar transport characteristics. The GQDs layer can substantially lower the exposure of BP surface to water molecules and oxygen. BP surface covered with GQDs shows no oxidation for up to 7 days as evidenced by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The electrical measurement and Raman spectra confirmed that the electrical and optical properties of BP-based device can be retained for more than 30 days with GQDs protection. On the other hand, oxidation protrusions on the BP surface without GQDs occur within a few days under same conditions. Encouragingly, we observed that along with protecting BP, a varnish of GQDs layer helps to further boost the electronic device parameters, including on/off ratio, on-current, and mobility. Moreover, with the GQDs on the top of BP surface, the electrical characteristics can be maintained for over 24 h under heating at 100 ℃ in ambient atmosphere, indicating high stability of our fabricated device. The current work proposes a facile approach for protection of BP via selectively decorating GQDs layers on its surface.
Citric acid and β-cyclodextrin were used as main precursors. Bulk BP crystals were purchased from HQ Graphene.

BP Nano flakes were mechanically exfoliated by tapes on poly-dimethyl-siloxane (PDMS) and transferred onto the silicon wafer containing 300 nm thick silicon dioxide dielectric layer in a nitrogen-filled glove box with oxygen and water contents of 0.1 and 0.01 ppm. The thickness of BP samples was estimated by optical microscope and AFM. Metal patterns were firstly deposited onto a silicon wafer by evaporating 60 nm gold with a Multi-chamber Vacuum Coating System (ASB-EPI-C6) and then peeled-off with PDMS in the glove box. Then, the metal pattern was transferred onto BP flakes adopting a standard transfer process.

GQDs were synthesized by one step hydrothermal method using citric acid and β-cyclodextrin as the main precursors. (ref. [42]) The prepared GQDs were diluted by ethanol and dropped onto the BP surface in ambient conditions. Then the samples were stored in an ambient tube atmosphere.

The optical images of the fabricated devices were collected with a Leica optical microscope. The electrical measurements were performed under vacuum conditions (10-5 m Torr) using a Keithley semiconductor parameter analyzer, 4200-SCS, with a Lakeshore probe station. The surface topography was collected by a Bruker Icon Atomic force microscope (AFM) operated in scanasyst mode. The AFM images were further processed with NanoScope Analysis software package to align adjacent rows. Confocal Raman spectroscopy was performed on a WITEC-Alpha-300R, the laser wavelength was 532 nm and the power was ~ 0.2 mW.

Few layer BP flakes were mechanically exfoliated from bulk BP (HQ Graphene) and transferred onto a 300 nm SiO2/Si substrate via scotch tape method in a nitrogen-filled glove box. Owing to the high on/off ratio, high mobility as well as the sensitivity to the ambient condition compared to the thicker flakes (around 10–20 nm), BP flakes with a thickness of less than 10 nm were selected to perform the experiment. Gold electrode pattern was made using the transfer method, as depicted in Fig. 1 a. The gold electrodes were firstly deposited onto the silicon with a mask using the electron beam vaporization (EBV) technique. Then, a clean PDMS was used to peel-off the metal patterns from silicon surface. After that, the metal patterns were relocated on the BP surface by transfer method to get the FET devices. Patterning electrodes directly (such as, through electron beam lithography) is a good fabrication approach, but it may introduce some interference (e.g., photoresist and other chemicals) during the deposition. By adopting transfer method, the exposure time of BP to the ambient atmosphere could be substantially reduced as well as improved contact between the active material and electrodes can be attained. All the FET fabrication process was performed in nitrogen-filled glove box.

The GQDs were prepared through a hydrothermal process, with the reaction between citric acid and β-cyclodextrin [42]. Before the GQDs were introduced onto BP surface, the original GQDs were diluted with pure ethanol. GQDs with an average size of around 5 nm were achieved that can be selectively adsorbed onto the BP surface, as illustrated in Fig. 1a. The inset in Fig. 1c shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GQDs. We observed that GQDs can form continuous layers with different thickness on mica depending upon the used concentration (Fig. S1). We found that it is difficult to control the exact thickness of the GQD layer on the surface of BP and also, GQDs tend to form islands sometimes. We employed an optimized concentration of GQDs (GQDs in ethanol with a concentration of 1:3) that was found to be suitable in finding a developed sample and protected the BP surface very well. AFM analysis was performed on BP samples to detect the layer thickness immediately after exposing to ambient atmosphere. As shown in Fig. Fig. Fig. 1b, the thickness of the bare BP is about 7.6 nm, corresponding to approximately 14 atomic layers [43]. Then, a diluted GQDs solution in ethanol was dropped onto the BP surface. Just after the treatment of BP's surface, another AFM measurement was performed to see the changes in the morphology of BP samples. The thickness was increased by around 0.6 nm (ref. [42]), indicating that one layer of GQDs was adsorbed onto BP surface. Through tuning the concentration of GQDs in ethanol solution, GQDs layer with different thickness can be obtained.

TEM imaging confirmed that a thin layer of GQDs has been successfully absorbed on BP surface. A flake of BP on TEM grid is shown in Fig. 2 a and a corresponding TEM image is demonstrated in Fig. 2b. Fig. 2c, d depict the presence of GQDs at the upper layer of BP surface at different resolution. GQDs can form a continuous layer with varying thickness. We can observe from Fig. 2c,d that GQDs with 0.6 nm thickness on the BP is brighter in TEM images than the counterparts with higher thickness in the range of 3–5 nm, which is ascribed to the fact that GQDs sometimes form islands (due to random adsorption at different places) that often appear as dark contrast. The varying contrast in the two images results from the different thickness of GQDs, higher the thickness darker the image, as explained above.

AFM was employed to examine the degradation process of BP surface. The AFM images of BP covered with GQDs taken on different days are shown in Fig. 3 (a–d) and the surface area selected for AFM analysis is highlighted with a red box in Fig. 5a. As can be observed in Fig. 3a, GQDs not only formed a layer on BP surface, but also many islands with an approximate thickness of 5 nm are grown. The GQDs covered surface of BP shows outstanding stability for a long period. Fig. 3(a–d) displayed the AFM measurement of BP surface performed in the first seven days, a corner area chosen from the BP FET device shown in Fig. 5a (red box). It can be seen from images that the BP surface covered with GQDs was hardly oxidized, and no oxide protrusion appeared, however, the GQDs layer became fuzzy as a result of AFM tip disturbance (Fig. 3d). On the other hand, AFM images of the degradation of bare BP surface after different days of formation are displayed in Fig. 3(e–h), indicating significant degradation and hence, revealing poor stability in atmosphere. Small bumps have appeared on the surface due to the oxidization just on the third day (Fig. 3f) of exposure. Due to the fast degradation of bare BP surface, the oxide protrusions become very obvious in seven days, as verified in Fig. 3g, causing the BP layer to vanish within two weeks of exposure in atmosphere (Fig. 3d). While, in the case of GQDs covered BP sample, no significant change is observed in its morphology even after 20 days of exposure to ambient atmosphere. The stability of BP layer could be maintained for around 5 weeks by decorating it with GQDs. Compared to the bare BP, although water molecules exist in the diluted GQDs, the formed GQDs layer on the BP surface keeps the oxygen away from BP surface and considerably slows down the oxidation process, which occurs with the coexistence of water and oxygen.44,45 However, after 30–35 days, the BP surface underwent some chemical changes due to the beginning of oxidation process, and the zoon with thin GQDs covered was firstly oxidized in Fig. S2.

We further carried out Raman characterization for the bare BP and the GQDs protected BP to reveal the degradation process. The Raman instrument was maintained in an identical situation throughout the experiments as BP has highly anisotropic optical responses. In Fig. 4 a, a comparison of Raman peaks of bare BP and GQDs covered BP is shown, exhibiting three characteristic peaks at 359, 439, and 465.15 cm−1, corresponding to the three vibrational modes of A1g, B2g, and A2g in BP crystal lattice, respectively. The intensity and peak position of all the three Raman peaks of BP covered with GQDs remained essentially unchanged for 35 days, indicating that BP was well protected with a slight degradation, and the corresponding AFM image in Fig. S3 also shows a slight change in its morphology after 35 days. After two weeks of exposure to ambient atmosphere, the A2g peak intensity of GQDs-covered BP was slightly reduced by 14.5% and red-shifted by 1.16 cm−1, suggesting the beginning of oxidation process [33,39,41,46]. In contrast, bare BP samples show an obvious transformation in Raman measurements within 3 days of exposure. On the 3rd day of exposure, the intensity of A2g peaks decreased by 30%and the band shifted by 1.71 cm−1 in Fig. 4b. Moreover, its Raman peaks vanished completely after 14 days of exposure in atmosphere under same conditions (Fig. 4a, d) [33]. Also, the relevant AFM images revealed serious oxidation of BP nanosheets (Fig. S3). The comparison of Raman mappings of A2g peak clearly indicates effective protection of BP with GQDs, which shows high intensity of A2g peak even after 35 days (Fig. 4c), whereas, almost no A2g peak signal is observed after 14 days in case of bare BP (Fig. 4d).

We then fabricated FETs with bare BP and GQDs-covered BP to evaluate the difference in their electrical characteristics and stability. All the electric measurements were conducted in high vacuum (10−5 m Torr) condition. Fig. 5 a displays the AFM image of GQDs-covered BP based FET. The thickness of BP nanosheet was measured to be approximately 6.3 nm. The Isd–Vsd characteristics of corresponding device (Fig. 5b) reveal excellent linearity in both electron (Vg from 0 to 50 V) and hole (Vg from −50 to 0 V) transport regime with Vsd sweeping from −1 to 1 V, indicating that a good ohmic contact exists between the gold electrode and the BP flake. Fig. 5c presents the transfer characteristic (Isd–Vg) of the same device in linear scale at Vsd = 1 V, demonstrating a typical ambipolar transport behaviour (black curve). In a gate voltage range of −50 to 50 V, the source-drain current increases along both positive and negative sweeping directions, representing the electron and hole transport, respectively. The current on/off ratio of larger than 3 × 103 can be clearly observed from the logarithmic plot in Fig. S4, which is consistent well with the previous reports [40,47]. The transfer curve showed a slight negative shift in the threshold voltage, as can be seen in Fig. 5c (red curve), comparing to the bare BP-based device, which is caused by doping of residual water molecules in the diluted solution [45]. The residual water molecules did not break the surface structure of BP for the reason that oxygen was separated by the GQDs. Unlike other chemical passivation strategies [23,28,44], the GQDs were physically adsorbed on BP surface and hence, the original properties of BP flakes were preserved. However, the transfer curve showed a positive shift in the threshold voltage after one day (Fig. 5c, S3) as the water molecules are entirely immersed, indicating the GQDs induced p-type doping effect on BP, where holes transferred from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of GQDs to the valence band of BP. However, with a passage of time, some surface chemical modification or covalent BP amendment via the formation of covalent P-C bonds could occur [28].

To evaluate the field-effect mobility of BP, following Equation was used by linear fitting of the transfer plot,
(1)
μ =
L
W
C i
V
sd
d
I
sd
d
V g
where L and W represent the length and width of conduction channel, respectively, Ci is the capacitance per unit area between BP and back gate calculated by Ci = ε0εr/d (εr : the dielectric constant; d : the thickness of SiO2), dIsd/dVg represents the slope of the linear regime in transfer curve. The final extracted hole and electron mobilities of BP were estimated to be 200.4 and 60.7 cm2V-1s−1, respectively. The field-effect mobility of GQDs-covered BP device increased slightly to 206 cm2V-1s−1 after one day of fabrication, which is attributed to the evaporation of water molecules that resulted in decreased surface scattering. In the case of pristine BP, there were many surface states, which can trap the charge carriers. By depositing GQDs on BP surface, the trapping states are bonded with GQDs, so the trapping effect can be reduced after GQDs passivation, resulting in the enhanced charge carrier mobility. With protection of GQDs layers, the BP can retain stability for more than one month, while its hole carrier mobility was decreased to be 75.3 cm2V-1s−1 since the surface was slightly oxidized (Fig. 5e). Meanwhile, the on/off ratio of the device increased one order (Fig. 5f) due to p-type doping. As an assessment, Fig. 5d displays the transfer characteristic (Isd–Vg) of a bare BP at Vsd = 1 V, measured after various storage time, which demonstrates a clear decrease in Isd as it stored longer, caused by the oxidation of BP surface. Meanwhile, the hole carrier mobility and the on/off ratio of unprotected BP device decreased quickly compared to the GQDs protected BP based counterpart (Fig. 5e-f), which are consistent with the AFM and Raman characterizations. These results clearly demonstrate the effectiveness of our surface passivation method to maintain the stability of BP in ambient condition.

We further investigated the movement of GQDs on BP surface and corresponding protection of BP under different heating conditions in atmosphere. By changing the concentration of GQDs in ethanol, we can modify its thickness but getting a highly thick GQDs layer on the surface of BP is a challenging process. Fig. 6 a displays the AFM image of a BP flake covered with a 4.2 nm thick GQDs layer (the thickness of pristine BP flake is 7.6 nm, as shown in Fig. 1b), from which some flaky islands can be detected. Firstly, we heated the sample at 20 ℃ for 30 mins and it did not show any significant change in GQDs layer thickness. After heating for 30 mins at 50 ℃, the thickness of GQDs layer remained to be about 4.1 nm (Fig. 6b), indicating a very minor change (0.1 nm) in the thickness of GQDs layer, the disappeared islands and flat surface indicates that GQDs move on the BP surface but will not vaporize at 50 ℃. However, the thickness of GQDs layer decreased to about 1.8 nm, corresponding to 2–3 layers of GQDs upon heating at 100 ℃ for 30 mins (Fig. 6c), implying the top GQDs would move away from the surface at this temperature since the size of GQDs is only about 5 nm. After increasing the heating temperature to 150 ℃ for 30 mins, only one tightly adsorbed GQDs layer was left on BP surface, which is consistent with a previous report on temperature etching of BP [48]. Meanwhile, the surface is still flat and no oxidation protrusion appears (Fig. 6d). The corresponding transfer curves of the same FET device at each heating temperature are shown in Fig. 6e. However, heating at a temperature of 200 ℃ for 30 mins in atmosphere broke the device, and a layer of oxidations left on the silicon (Fig. S5). We further demonstrate that when the device based on GQDs covered BP is continuously heated for 24 h at 100 ℃, it still shows good electrical performance, indicating high stability and excellent protection compared to a similar experiment performed on bare BP based FET which exhibited no current after heat treatment for 24 h at 100 ℃ (Fig. 6f). Further investigation is needed to explore a method to facilely cover larger sized BP surface for its effective protection under higher temperature conditions for certain applications.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated a new facile method to protect BP device from atmosphere by utilizing selectively adsorbed GQDs on BP surface that resulted in outstanding stability and improved electrical performance of our optimized FET. By performing in-depth investigation via AFM analysis, Raman spectroscopy and electrical measurements, it is confirmed that the stability of GQDs protected BP can be significantly improved. This method offers a feasible and effective way for surface protection of BP to realize improved stability as well as high on/off ratio and high current mobility. Moreover, the electrical properties of our optimized FETs could be retained for over 24 h even under heating at 100 ℃ in ambient conditions. We anticipate that our findings would further encourage the development of facile and cost-effective approaches to prevent the degradation of BP and other air-sensitive materials while maintaining their intrinsic characteristics for the realization of practical applications.

Professor Wenjing Zhang supervised the whole project and provided resources. Vijay Laxmi and W eilong Dong conceived the main conceptual idea as well as performed the experiments and designed the outlines of manuscript. H aoqiang Wang and Qunhui Yuan contributed to the Graphene Quantum Dots formation. Vijay Laxmi and W eilong Dong wrote the manuscript with the support of Waqas Ahmad and Muhammad Najeeb Ullah Shah, and contributed to the analysis part. Dianyu Qi, Qiaoyan Hao and Zhengbiao Ouyang helped for editing and shaping the final version of manuscript. All authors facilitated and assisted in the research, analysis and preparation of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work at Shenzhen University (SZU) was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51472164 and 61805159), the 1000 Talents Program for Young Scientists of China, Shenzhen Peacock Plan (Grant No. KQTD2016053112042971), the Educational Commission of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2015KGJHZ006 and 2016KCXTD006), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2016B050501005), Natural Science Foundation of SZU (Grant No. 000050).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148089.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Black phosphorus and its isoelectronic materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xia, F., Wang, H., Hwang, J. C. M., Neto, A. H. C. & Yang, L. Black phosphorus and its isoelectronic materials. Nature Reviews Physics 1, 306-317, doi:10.1038/s42254-019-0043-5 (2019).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Layer-dependent band alignment and work function of few-layer phosphorene

Das, S. et al. Tunable transport gap in phosphorene. Nano letters 14, 5733-5739 (2014).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fast and broadband photoresponse of few-layer black phosphorus field-effect transistors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Widely tunable black phosphorus mid-infrared photodetector
